note: Original synthetic test corpus in the wall-to-wall bombastic-narrator style. Purpose-built benchmark data, entirely fictional, containing no text from any copyrighted work. Recurring elements: Wendell the Wick-Trimmer, Ilsa the Rope-Widow, and the Lens — a lighthouse lens of unclear intentions.

segments.text: Dear listener! Behold Port Havelock, a fistful of rooftops flung at the cliffs like an apology. And there — yes, there, trudging up the harbor stairs — comes our hero, Wendell the Wick-Trimmer, a man whose destiny is enormous and whose boots are more so.
segments.scene: Aerial shot of a cliffside harbor village at dusk; a man in oversized boots climbs stone stairs.

segments.text: Wendell climbs the lighthouse stairs, each step a small negotiation with his lungs. He is thinking, as he always thinks, of the Lens. The Lens that hums. The Lens that knows.
segments.scene: The man ascends a spiral lighthouse staircase, lantern in hand.

segments.text: And now — Ilsa! Ilsa the Rope-Widow, splicer of lines and of reputations! She enters the harbor office the way a tide enters a cellar: unasked, total, and carrying a boat hook. Ladies and gentlemen, if you have never been loved by a terrifying aunt, I pity you. I pity you into the sea.
segments.scene: An older woman bursts through a harbor office door holding a boat hook.

segments.text: The Lens hums a low B-flat, which as any drowned sailor will tell you is the note of unfinished business.
segments.scene: Close-up of a huge lighthouse lens in the lamp room, faintly vibrating.

segments.text: Wendell reaches out one trembling finger — the finger of a man who has never once won an argument — and touches the glass. Friends, I must speak plainly. There are lamps that light harbors, and there are lamps that light something older, lamps ground so fine they remember when the sea was young and merciful. This is the second kind.
segments.scene: The man touches the lens; dust rises; the humming intensifies.

segments.text: The hatch slams! Ilsa stands in it like a government. 'Nobody climbs the tower after the equinox,' she does not say, because her eyes say it, and her eyes have tenure.
segments.scene: The aunt appears in the lamp-room hatchway, backlit, boat hook lowered.

segments.text: Now the villagers assemble on the quay, bearing lanterns and casseroles, for in Port Havelock every emergency is also a potluck. The harbormaster climbs onto a crab crate, clears his throat for what history will record as too long, and declares the situation 'basically fine' — the three most cursed words in any language.
segments.scene: Villagers gather at night on the quay with lanterns and covered dishes; a stout man stands on a crate addressing them.

segments.text: Wendell runs. Understand: this is not athletic running. This is furniture escaping a house fire. His mighty boots surrender at the ankle.
segments.scene: The man sprints awkwardly down the lighthouse stairs, boots flying off.

segments.text: And the Lens rises. Dear listener, I have narrated shipwrecks. I have narrated christenings, which are worse. But I have never narrated a half-ton of grinding glass ascending a stairwell with the patience of an institution, and I confess my voice is not prepared. Nobody's voice is prepared.
segments.scene: The great lens floats slowly up the lighthouse stairwell by itself, glowing faintly.

segments.text: Ilsa steps forward. She has buried three husbands, two of them deceased. She raises the boat hook — the one the family calls Grandfather — and addresses the Lens in the old dialect, which sounds like an anchor apologizing.
segments.scene: The aunt confronts the glowing lens on the quay, boat hook raised, speaking.

segments.text: The moon comes out from behind the clouds, enormous and pale, and every villager thinks the same thought at the same moment, which the church bells confirm: it is a lens. It has always been a lens.
segments.scene: A huge pale full moon emerges; villagers look up in horror; bells ring.

segments.text: And so Wendell the Wick-Trimmer, bootless and radiant, stands between his village and the humming light, and finds — where his fear should be — a purpose. Roll credits? No. No, dear listener. Roll nothing. We are just getting started.
segments.scene: The man stands facing the glowing lens and the moon, villagers behind him; the shot holds.
